trajectory_summary: The call opened with a warm, cooperative customer who had done genuine research and was giving Ion a final look. Randy completed verify competently and held setter scope when pressed on incentives. However, he never deployed a curiosity-creation or comparison-framing move against Kurt's repeated pricing requests and explicit competitor preference, and the call ended without a booked appointment — Kurt deferred to a future callback contingent on sending the bill. The outcome is tentative and customer-gated, not a confirmed sit.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct classification is tentative/conditional, not booked. Kurt explicitly declined to set a time on the call ('I'll give you guys a callback to set a a time') and made the next step contingent on his own action (sending the bill). Per schema analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time is materially weaker than a confirmed appointment. The outcome should be tagged as callback_pending, not appointment_booked.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up execution — locking a time before ending the call
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Once I reply to you and we go from there, and I'll I'll let you know. K. I'll give you guys a callback to set a a time since you want me here first time.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Randy made one time attempt at 11:14 ('do you wanna shoot for tomorrow morning?') but when Kurt deferred, Randy accepted the soft outcome without cycling through any button-up protocol elements. On a paid lead with a warm, cooperative customer who has done active research, the failure to lock a time is a direct paycheck risk — a callback-pending outcome that Kurt controls is not a sit. Walk Randy through the full button-up checklist against this call and identify the specific moment where a second time-lock attempt was available.

key_moments.quote: Great. So I just wanted to confirm a few quick details here with you real quick. I'll keep this call super short for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy never names Ion Solar, never references how he got Kurt's information, and never establishes the legitimacy frame the schema requires in the first 30 seconds. He moves directly from confirming Kurt's identity into verify. Kurt's warm opening (the Kenny name-check) masked the gap — the call proceeded anyway — but the intro beat was skipped entirely. This is a procedural miss, not a strategic choice.

key_moments.quote: Well, if it's, alright. If I bought it, it, fourteen, fifteen years. So I was gonna say sixteen years because it was just put on prior to the sale. And and a thirty year warranty on this roof.
key_moments.stewart_read: The schema flags roof age over 15 years with no replacement planned as a hard DQ risk. Kurt's roof is 15-16 years old — right at the threshold. Kurt volunteers a 30-year warranty claim, which mitigates the concern, but Randy never probes whether that warranty is transferable to Kurt as the buyer, what condition the roof is in after 15 years, or whether any replacement is planned. The warranty claim is taken at face value and the conversation moves on. This is a DQ-adjacent gap: not a confirmed disqualifier, but an unresolved flag that the closer will need to address.

key_moments.quote: I was just actually went online today through the VA, the veterans, and they were telling me who to go with and who not to go with. So I I'm just wanna get a a rough idea on pricing.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kurt is a veteran who consulted the VA's solar guidance the same day as this call. This is an active decision window signal — he is not casually curious, he is in research mode with institutional backing. Randy acknowledges the pricing request with a scope-hold ('solar is different for everyone') but does not anchor on the VA research as a trust-building moment or use it to create curiosity about what Ion's design would show specifically for Kurt's home. The enthusiasm signal — a veteran with VA guidance actively comparing options today — was walked past.

key_moments.quote: Yes. You are. I get I get paid by the hour, so it's really no no problem. Take all the time you need.
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy's 'I get paid by the hour' line is a rapport move — it signals patience and reduces Kurt's anxiety about taking time on the phone. It landed well (Kurt laughs and continues navigating the app). This is a genuine micro-win: Randy read the moment correctly and used humor to hold the customer through a friction point. Worth naming as a positive in the 1-on-1.
